video_name: Video_19_Tool Calling in LangChain ｜ Generative AI using LangChain ｜ Video 17 ｜ CampusX.mp3
full_text: Hi guys, my name is Nitesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel. In this video also we will continue our Lang chain playlist. If you remember, in the last video we started a new section in this playlist where we will learn to make AI agents with the help of Lang chain. And I wanted to do this whole work step by step. And that is why in the last video I told you a very important concept whose name was tools. Now today's video is the continuation of the last video where we will read another concept which we call tool calling. And trust me guys, tool calling is a very important concept if you talk about AI agents. So on the whole, in this video we will understand this whole concept in detail and practically implement it. And lastly, I will show you an application where we will do real-time currency conversion with the help of a LLM. So the video will be very interesting and for the next videos it will be a very fundamental topic. So please make sure you watch this video till the end. Now let's start the video. Before starting the video, I would like to give you a quick revision of the last video. So in the last video we discussed that LLMs have two very big powers. The first power is of reasoning. Which means that if you give a question to an LLM, then the LLM can break down that question and understand what is being asked. Right? And the second power is output generation. So based on what is being asked, the LLM accesses its parametric knowledge and generates an answer in the output. So in nutshell, the two things that the LLM does very well are understanding the question of the other person, reasoning, and second, based on the question, generating the right output. So you can take the LLM like a human being who is good at thinking as well as good at speaking. Right? But the biggest problem of LLMs is that LLMs cannot give you things by doing them. They can generate output, they can generate text, but if you ask them to go to my database and make these changes, then the LLM cannot do that. If you ask them to post on LinkedIn or Twitter on my behalf, then the LLM cannot do that. If you ask them to go and hit an API and find out the current weather conditions of Delhi, then the LLM cannot do that. So basically, LLMs, although they are good at reasoning and output generation, they cannot do tasks on their own. Again, if you go to that human analogy, then LLMs are like human beings who can think and speak, but they don't have hands or feet to do anything. And this is a very big problem. If you really want to make LLMs a very powerful entity, an entity that performs tasks in the software world, then it's very very important that LLMs have hands and feet in some way so that they can carry out different types of tasks. And then in the last video, we saw the solution to this problem. And what was the solution? The solution was tools. We discussed that what you can do is you can explicitly create some tools and every tool has a task to carry out. Like I showed you a tool in the last video called DuckDuckGoSearch. This was a special tool whose job is that if you use it, then it can go to DuckDuckGoSearch engine and search anything. Similarly, I showed you the Shell tool which can run command lines and commands. And you can create tools according to your work. Now these tools are essentially functions that we have read in Python. These are special functions that have the capability that they can interact with LLMs when needed. So we had a very deep discussion about these tools in the last video about how tools are made, what are built-in tools, what are custom tools, what are toolkits. But we did not discuss this in the last video that if you have a tool and an LLM, then how will you connect them? This discussion was left for us. And we did not discuss that how an LLM calls a tool when needed. So this is the topic of our today's video. If we have an LLM and a tool, then we will learn how to connect these two. And when these two are connected, I will show you how an LLM can call a tool when needed. So that it can carry out any task that it wants to. So these are the two things that we will learn in today's video. I hope you got the context of the last video and you understood what we are going to do in today's video. Now we will talk about tool binding. So tool binding is the process where you connect your tool with your LLM. Or in other words, you register a tool with an LLM. Okay? It is written here. Tool binding is the step where you register tools with a large language model. So that the LLM knows what tools are available. It also knows what each tool does. And it also knows what input format to use. Okay? So I told you in the last video that while making every tool, you have to tell some things. Like what is the name of that tool. Second, you have to give a description of that tool. So that the LLM understands what that tool does. And third, you have to make an input schema of every tool. That in which format that tool is expecting input. So when you do tool binding, then there are two or three things with the help of tool binding. First, your LLM knows what tools it has. Which it can use. Second, it understands the work of every tool. Because it has a description. And third, the LLM also understands how to invoke every tool. That means in which format every tool is expecting input. As soon as LLM understands this, then in future, when it will call that tool, then it will send input in the same format in which the tool is expecting. So all this work is done in this particular step. Which we call tool binding. Okay? So let's do one thing. This is a very practical concept. There will be no benefit in reading it in theory. Along with that, I will code it. So what I will do now is, I will write a code where I will make a small tool. And then I will bind that tool with LLM. Let's go to Google Colab. So this is my Google Colab notebook. Here is my OpenAI secret key. Don't worry, I have trimmed this. It won't work if you run it exactly. I have installed some libraries here. And I have imported those libraries. Okay? Now we will start our work from here. First of all, we will create a tool. So again, we are making the same simple multiplication tool. Because we are just understanding the concept. Going forward in the video, I will make a difficult type of tool for you. So to make the tool, we are using tool decorator. And the name of this tool is multiply. And it needs A, which will be integer. B, which will be integer again. And the output from this tool will be integer. And here we will have to add a description. Given two numbers A and B, this tool returns their product. Okay? And here we are returning A times B. Okay? So this is my tool. Right? I had shown you that this tool is ready. So you can invoke it. Because after all, this is a runnable. So here we are sending 3 in dictionary, and 4 in B. And you can see we are getting 12. Along with that, if you fetch .name attribute, you will get the name of this tool. If you fetch the description attribute, you will get the description of this tool. And if you fetch the args parameter, you will get the schema of the tool and the input format it is expecting. Okay? So we have created the tool. Now comes the very important step, where we will bind the tool. Okay? To do this, we need an LLM. So I am creating an LLM, Chat OpenAI. Okay? Now this is the most important step. This LLM has a function, which is called bind tools. Now in this bind tools, you can bind any number of tools in a list. For example, I have only one tool. So I am writing its name. But if I had more than one tool, I could add more tools by writing comma. And now I have run it. And you can see it is binded. Okay? The only thing is that you have to store this result in a new variable. So I am giving it the name of a variable, LLM with tools. Okay? Now this LLM with tools is an LLM, but it also has a tool, a multiply tool. And now what will happen is that whenever this LLM will feel that it has to do multiplication in the future, then this LLM can call that tool, the multiply tool. Okay? So this piece of code that I showed you, that's all that happens is tool binding. This is what tool binding is. Okay? Now I would like to tell you one thing here. That every LLM does not have the capability that you can bind a tool with it. There are only a few LLMs who have this capability. I will show you a list later. In which you will get the name of all those LLMs where you can do tool binding. Or in short, that LLM can work with tools. Okay? So yeah, this was the tool binding in nutshell. A process where you connect your LLM with a given tool. Now we have learned two very important concepts. We learned to make a tool and we learned to connect a tool with an LLM. Now comes the most important third work where we will see when and how a tool is called when a LLM is needed. And this process is called tool calling. See, it is written here. Tool calling is a process where the LLM decides during a conversation or task that it needs to use a specific tool and generates a structured output with the name of the tool and the arguments to call it with. Okay? So the basic idea is that let's take a scenario where we are talking to an LLM and we have given that LLM access to a tool. Let's say, we have given access to a multiplication tool. Now during the conversation, if you ask that LLM normal questions like Hi, how are you? So at this point, for this particular question, LLM will not call any tool because it knows that it can do this work without any tool. So it will simply reply to you, Hi, I am good, how can I help you? Now let's say if a question comes from the front, that what's 8 multiplied by 7? So at this point, LLM will go and check what available tools it has. Now it knows that it has a tool called multiply. So what will it do? It will generate a structured output where it will first print the name of the tool. That the name of the tool is multiply. And at the same time, it will generate a schema in which it will tell what to send to the input of this multiply tool. So since LLM had asked you in the question that how much is 8 multiplied by 7? So this argument will generate a schema and give your LLM. It will say that A is 8 and B is 7. So this exact thing will be shown to you in the output. Rather than a normal text output. And this process is called tool calling. So I hope you understood what tool calling is. Now I will show you again through code. We have just created a tool a while ago. And we have bind that tool with our LLM. Now see what I am doing. I am using this LLM with tools. Now suppose I asked a simple question. Hi, how are you? And I will run this. Now I got an AI message. Where it is written in the content. Hello, I am here to help you with. But you will not see this thing anywhere. Why? Because for this given query, LLM did not feel that it needs to use any tool. But now see, If I change this query. And write here. Can you multiply 3 with 10? And now I will run this. So now you will notice that the content in the AI message is empty. See here in the content, You could see this thing. But the content is empty. But you will not see this thing. And that is this. Let's call this tool call. Focus till here. As much as I have selected. So in this tool call, You can see multiple tool calls in a list. For now, there is only one tool call. So what I can do is, I can directly fetch tool calls. Because this is an attribute. See here, This is our tool call. This is a list, There could be more than one tool call. But for now, there is only one tool call. And that is this. So what I can do is, I will show you the 0th item of this list. And see here guys, Now this dictionary that you can see, And this one that I showed you here, This will look similar to you. See here, you can see the name of the tool. That automatically LLM thought, That this particular tool can be helpful, In order to solve this query. So it told the name of the tool. And second, It also told me, What input should be sent to this tool, The multiply tool. So in the input, We have kept the value of A as 3 and B as 10. Apart from this, you can see one or two more things. You can see an ID of the tool call. This is a unique ID for every tool call. And type is tool call. So you can ignore these two things. These are the two main things. Now here, I know you are having a doubt in your mind. In your mind, You must be thinking that, We thought that Tool calling means, That LLM will turn this tool and call it. Meaning, it will invoke this tool And will bring the answer. But it is not like that. See here, something is written here. The LLM does not actually run the tool. It just suggests The tool and the input arguments. The actual execution is handled by Langchain or the programmer. The actual execution is handled by Langchain or the programmer. The actual execution is handled by Langchain or the programmer. Meaning, LLM never calls the tool on your behalf. LLM never calls the tool on your behalf. It only suggests to you, That look, I think this tool will be helpful To solve this query. To solve this query. And you send this input to this tool. And you send this input to this tool. But LLM will never call that tool. But LLM will never call that tool. It will not send the input by itself. It will not bring the result by itself. And this is logical too. Think about it. If LLM starts calling the tool on your behalf And starts executing it, Then this can be very risky. Because you cannot trust LLM too much. Because you cannot trust LLM too much. It may call a tool And it may be wrong. And it may bring the result. So you want to keep this control with you. You are just asking LLM for advice. That look, On the basis of this query, You tell me which tool you think will be right To find the answer. And also tell me the input. So that I send the input as it is to the tool. And bring the result back to you. But LLM does not execute the tool. But LLM does not execute the tool. So this is a big confusion In beginners that tool calling means That LLM is executing the tool. No. LLM is just advising you That for this given query This tool will be right With these input parameters. So I hope I was able to explain What tool calling is. Now I know you will feel That this is cheating. We have not seen the whole thing yet. So don't worry. Now I will show you the fourth thing. We saw tool creation, tool binding, Tool calling. Now I will show you the fourth thing. Tool execution. Where we will take this dictionary And with the help of this We will execute the tool. So we have learned three very powerful things. We have learned to create the tool. We have learned to bind the tool. And we have learned the concept of tool calling. Now comes the fourth And probably The most interesting piece That is tool execution. So once As LLM suggests That to answer this query You can use this tool And please While using this tool Send these inputs So now what you do You call that tool As a programmer Meaning you execute it And send the same inputs That LLM had told you And then tool gets executed And the result is This whole process is called Tool execution. Here it is written Tool execution is the step where The actual Python function Or tool is run Using the input arguments That the LLM suggested During tool calling. Like LLM said Call the multiply tool And tell the value of A as 8 And B as 7 And send the same value Of A and B as LLM had told you And then you will get the answer By turning it. So this whole process is called Tool execution. Again let's do it practically So at this point I have this tool call available Now with the help of this tool call What I will do I will do tool execution. So I know that My tool is called multiply And to execute it I have invoke function Now see what I have to send In invoke I have to send arguments So I will do one thing First of all I will store this Means if you send this whole thing Then see what happens What I am doing is I am sending this whole thing as input to my tool See what will happen this time This time also I am getting the result But chunks.text: Hi guys, my name is Nitesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel.
In this video also we will continue our Lang chain playlist.
If you remember, in the last video we started a new section in this playlist
where we will learn to make AI agents with the help of Lang chain.
And I wanted to do this whole work step by step.
And that is why in the last video I told you a very important concept
Now today's video is the continuation of the last video where we will read another concept
And trust me guys, tool calling is a very important concept if you talk about AI agents.
So on the whole, in this video we will understand this whole concept in detail
And lastly, I will show you an application where we will do real-time currency conversion
with the help of a LLM.
So the video will be very interesting and for the next videos it will be a very fundamental topic.
So please make sure you watch this video till the end.
Before starting the video, I would like to give you a quick revision of the last video.
So in the last video we discussed that LLMs have two very big powers.
The first power is of reasoning.
Which means that if you give a question to an LLM, then the LLM can break down that question
and understand what is being asked.
And the second power is output generation.
So based on what is being asked, the LLM accesses its parametric knowledge
and generates an answer in the output.
So in nutshell, the two things that the LLM does very well are
understanding the question of the other person, reasoning,
and second, based on the question, generating the right output.
So you can take the LLM like a human being who is good at thinking as well as good at speaking.
But the biggest problem of LLMs is that LLMs cannot give you things by doing them.
They can generate output, they can generate text,
but if you ask them to go to my database and make these changes,
then the LLM cannot do that.
If you ask them to post on LinkedIn or Twitter on my behalf,
then the LLM cannot do that.
If you ask them to go and hit an API and find out the current weather conditions of Delhi,
then the LLM cannot do that.
So basically, LLMs, although they are good at reasoning and output generation,
they cannot do tasks on their own.
Again, if you go to that human analogy,
then LLMs are like human beings who can think and speak,
but they don't have hands or feet to do anything.
And this is a very big problem.
If you really want to make LLMs a very powerful entity,
an entity that performs tasks in the software world,
then it's very very important that LLMs have hands and feet in some way
so that they can carry out different types of tasks.
And then in the last video, we saw the solution to this problem.
We discussed that what you can do is you can explicitly create some tools
and every tool has a task to carry out.
Like I showed you a tool in the last video called DuckDuckGoSearch.
This was a special tool whose job is that if you use it,
then it can go to DuckDuckGoSearch engine and search anything.
Similarly, I showed you the Shell tool
which can run command lines and commands.
And you can create tools according to your work.
Now these tools are essentially functions that we have read in Python.
These are special functions that have the capability
that they can interact with LLMs when needed.
So we had a very deep discussion about these tools in the last video
about how tools are made, what are built-in tools,
what are custom tools, what are toolkits.
But we did not discuss this in the last video
that if you have a tool and an LLM, then how will you connect them?
This discussion was left for us.
And we did not discuss that how an LLM calls a tool when needed.
So this is the topic of our today's video.
If we have an LLM and a tool,
then we will learn how to connect these two.
And when these two are connected,
I will show you how an LLM can call a tool when needed.
So that it can carry out any task that it wants to.
So these are the two things that we will learn in today's video.
I hope you got the context of the last video
and you understood what we are going to do in today's video.
Now we will talk about tool binding.
So tool binding is the process where you connect your tool with your LLM.
Or in other words, you register a tool with an LLM.
Tool binding is the step where you register tools with a large language model.
So that the LLM knows what tools are available.
It also knows what each tool does.
And it also knows what input format to use.
So I told you in the last video that
while making every tool, you have to tell some things.
Like what is the name of that tool.
Second, you have to give a description of that tool.
So that the LLM understands what that tool does.
And third, you have to make an input schema of every tool.
That in which format that tool is expecting input.
So when you do tool binding,
then there are two or three things with the help of tool binding.
First, your LLM knows what tools it has.
Second, it understands the work of every tool.
And third, the LLM also understands how to invoke every tool.
That means in which format every tool is expecting input.
As soon as LLM understands this,
then in future, when it will call that tool,
then it will send input in the same format in which the tool is expecting.
